notes:
A lazy, otaku-type guy who is obsessed with a tragic dating sim gets reincarnated into the game world as the villainess — the "fake saint" who is destined to torment the true heroine and meet a bad end. Knowing the original plot, he decides to act as the perfect saint to fix everything: secure the heroine’s happy ending with the protagonist, defeat the big threat, and avoid getting exposed or killed. Outwardly she becomes an adored, flawless saint, when in front of people she act like the saint she is. but inside she’s still the same perverted, sarcastic, lazy, and occasionally sadistic guy he is.Comedic Engine
The main humor comes from massive misunderstandings and dramatic irony. The protagonist’s inner thoughts are selfish, flippant, perverted, or outright trollish, while every action is interpreted by others as profound wisdom, pure selflessness, or miraculous heroism. This gap between her real personality and the saintly image she projects drives most of the comedy.  Her “good” deeds often have pragmatic, otaku-knowledge-based, or self-serving reasons behind them, yet they get mythologized. The story leans heavily on gap moe, unreliable narration, absurd situations, and the side characters’ increasingly over-the-top worship of her.POV and Writing Style  Primarily first-person from the protagonist’s perspective (snarky, fun, very unreliable inner monologue).  
Frequently switches to third-person viewpoints of other characters.
This multi-POV structure is used cleverly to show the same events from wildly different angles, heightening the irony and comedy while giving emotional depth. The writing is straightforward, comedic, and fast-paced with good timing for jokes and escalation.

Arcs and Structure
The story roughly follows the game’s timeline across about one year but diverges more and more due to the protagonist’s actions. It includes:  Early setup and building her saint persona.  
Academy and tournament sections.  
Larger-scale efforts involving politics, monster subjugation, church/knights, and world-saving.  
Escalating mysteries about the saint/witch cycle and her own existence.  
Character growth, relationship-building, and a conclusive final confrontation.

It mixes fantasy action, academy life, heavy misunderstanding comedy, and some darker or emotional moments. The main story has a clear ending with thematic payoff, plus side stories afterward.

Preferred interiority level: HIGH — regardless of grammatical POV

What this means in practice:


The reader should get close to unfiltered access to the current focus character real-time thoughts: reactions, sarcasm, panic, half-formed reasoning — not a clean summary of what they felt after the fact.
Test sentence: does the prose ever just drop a raw thought straight into the narration, like dialogue ("What do we do?", "That was... not good.") — or does it always wrap thought in a reporting phrase ("she thought," "he felt")? The former = high interiority. The latter, used constantly, = low interiority, even in first person.
